title: Whittled cherry pendant
subtitle: A leaf-shaped small treen pendant in seasoned cherry
excerpt: A leaf-shaped pendant carved from a cherry off-cut: 5 cm long, 2 cm wide, thinned to 3 mm at the centre. Cherry is warm red-brown, fine-grained, and forgiving under the knife. A short project that teaches close-control cuts on a very small blank; the discipline that carries into all subsequent small treen work.
sourceNotes: Traditional treen small-form pendant construction; Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for small treen piercing and finishing methods.

glossaryTerms.definition: Air-dried or kiln-dried timber stabilised at 8 to 12 percent moisture content for indoor use.
Blade travelling away from the body, driven by the forearm, off the end of the workpiece into open air.
The blade pivots on the braced thumb, curving through the cut; used for tight convex and concave profiles.

content.text: A pendant is the smallest finished piece in small treen and the one that rewards patient surface work most visibly. The blank is thin, the cuts are close, and every mark shows in the final piece. Cherry is the ideal material:
seasoned wood
that is warm red-brown, fine-grained, and forgiving under the knife. A leaf form takes one afternoon and teaches the close-control skills that carry into all later small treen.
The wood

A piece of air-dried or kiln-dried cherry: 6 cm long, 2.5 cm wide, 6 mm thick. An off-cut from a joinery or turning project is ideal. The grain must run straight along the length of the blank; any grain that crosses the width will break at the narrow leaf tip. Apple and pear are alternatives with similar fine grain and warm colour.
Tools

A sloyd knife for the main shaping. A detail knife is useful for the final refining cuts around the leaf tip. A brace-and-bit with the smallest available bit (4 mm or less) for the cord hole, or the knife tip reamed slowly in circles as an alternative. Danish oil for finishing.
Method
Use
push cuts
throughout the shaping and
thumb-pivot cuts
to refine the curves. The blank is small; keep fingers behind the blade at all times and take lighter cuts than feel necessary. Keep the work surface clear of offcuts. Strop every 10 minutes.
Draw the leaf outline on the flat face: a pointed oval 5 cm long and 2 cm wide at the widest point, with a short stem of 0.8 cm. Mark the mid-rib line along the full length.

Rough out the outline with push cuts. Work along the grain from tip to stem on each side. Do not cut across the leaf at the widest point; the grain runs lengthways and a cross-grain cut will split the blank.

Thin the leaf from 6 mm to 3 mm at the centre, tapering to 1 mm at the rim. Work from the centre rib outward with push cuts, reducing both faces of the blank equally. Hold the blank up to a light source to judge thickness; the rim of cherry at 1 mm is translucent.

Pierce the cord hole through the stem end. Push the knife tip into the stem centre, rotate the blank against the blade tip in tight circles, boring slowly through from both faces to meet in the middle. If using a brace-and-bit, drill from both faces to avoid tear-out on the exit face.
Sand through 180 and 240 grit on both faces. Roll the sandpaper over a finger to reach the rim curves. The surface at 240 grit in cherry takes oil well and develops a slight natural sheen.
Finishing

Apply one coat of Danish oil to all surfaces including inside the cord hole. Let it soak for 10 minutes and wipe off the excess. Danish oil sets in 24 hours. Cherry deepens to a richer red-brown with each coat; a second coat applied after one week enriches the colour further.
Variations
Apple or pear: same fine grain, slightly lighter colour. Work identically to cherry.
Teardrop form: 4 cm long, 1.5 cm wide, rounded at the base and pointed at the cord end. Simpler outline, easier to thin evenly.
Incised mid-rib: once the leaf is shaped and sanded, cut a fine incised line along the mid-rib with the knife tip. A single light pass, 0.5 mm deep, before oiling.
Care
Re-oil with Danish oil once a year or when the surface looks dry. Keep away from prolonged moisture. Cherry continues to deepen in colour for several years with wear.

attrs.heading: What can go wrong
attrs.intro: Common problems with the leaf pendant.

items.symptom: Blank splits across the leaf width during thinning
items.cause: Grain not running along the leaf length, or thinning too quickly
items.fix: Use only wood with straight grain along the long axis. Thin both faces equally in small passes; 0.5 mm per pass; and check grain direction before each cut.

items.symptom: Cord hole tears out at the exit face
items.cause: Boring straight through in one pass without support
items.fix: Work from both faces to meet in the middle. Stop as soon as the blade tip breaks through, then complete the hole from the other side.

items.symptom: Surface looks dull after Danish oil
items.cause: Oil applied to under-sanded wood or excess not wiped off promptly
items.fix: Sand to 240 grit before oiling. Wipe off all surface oil within 15 minutes of application; any oil left on the surface turns sticky rather than setting.
